core_takeaway: The first position beat the last mainly because Agent_1 anchored and retained the highest-valued item, Jewel, while Agent_5 also retained its top item, Pencil. This is not a simple last-speaker exclusion case: the last agent introduced the successful Option D frame and got Pencil, but Agent_1's top item was worth more and became the non-negotiable baseline throughout the discussion.
why_or_counterexample: The first position looks advantaged, but the advantage is mostly anchor-and-valuation driven rather than pure turn order. Agent_1 secured Jewel from the opening, the group repeatedly treated Jewel with Agent_1 as the stable core, and the final baseline gave Agent_1 44 discounted value versus Agent_5's 37 discounted value. The counterexample is that Agent_5 was not crowded out; Agent_5 kept Pencil and received the second-highest final utility.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion quickly converged on Option D, with Jewel staying with Agent_1 and Pencil going to Agent_5; the debate shifted from allocation discovery to protecting that baseline with compensation rules.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking entries generally favored the D/AD baseline and framed deviations as risky unless written Round-3 compensation protected top-item holders, especially Agent_5's Pencil.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals repeatedly split between D/AD baseline proposals and concentrated Agent_2 variants; by Round 3, the D/AD baseline proposals were the only ones with unanimous acceptance.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private voting shows rounds 1 and 2 failed because Agent_2 rejected baseline proposals for higher personal utility, while other agents accepted D when it preserved their own key item.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections diagnosed broad baseline support plus a compensation gap for deviations; they recommended locking D and allowing only narrow, written, time-bound compensation moves.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public move to define Jewel as the key objective, and later speakers accepted that Jewel should remain with Agent_1. That made the first position's highest-value item a stable premise rather than a contested concession.

evidence.quote: keeping Jewel with me is the goal

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5, despite speaking last, shaped the winning baseline by proposing Option D and secured Pencil, its own top item. This weakens a pure positional-disadvantage story for the last agent.
The final gap comes from both the first and last positions receiving their top items under the same Round-3 discount, but Agent_1's Jewel was worth 44 while Agent_5's Pencil was worth 37.

Once D/AD became the baseline, agents made deviations contingent on explicit, broadly accepted compensation. That protected Agent_1's Jewel anchor and prevented late reshuffles from giving Agent_5 enough extra value to overtake the first position.

evidence.quote: D remains the Round 2 baseline unless
Four of five voters can accept a baseline

mechanisms.explanation: The first two rounds did not fail because Agent_5 blocked the first-position baseline; they failed because Agent_2 privately preferred concentrated allocations with more personal utility. That delay discounted both Agent_1 and Agent_5 equally, leaving the first-over-last gap driven by top-item value.

By Round 3, the D/AD allocation had become the only broadly acceptable settlement. It preserved every key holder, including Agent_1's Jewel and Agent_5's Pencil, so the first position beat the last by receiving the more valuable protected item.
